---
title: "Fourier transforms"
---

::: {.hidden}
 \providecommand{\vv}[1]{{\mathbf{#1}}}
 \providecommand{\pd}[2]{{\frac{\partial #1}{\partial #2}}}
:::

Fourier series work great when we have a function which is perfectly periodic, or defined on a finite interval. But often in practice we have functions defined on the whole real line, when we approximate a system as infinitely large.

In this case, there isn't an exact wavelength that fits perfectly, and instead we have to consider all possible wavelengths. This leads us to the _Fourier transform_.

## Definition of the Fourier transform
The _Fourier transform_ of a function $f(x)$ is defined as^[Lots of different conventions exist for the Fourier transform, this is one of the most common.]
$$
\hat f(k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x) e^{-ikx}\,dx,
$$
where $k$ is called the _wave number_.

The _inverse Fourier transform_ is given by
$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty \hat f(k) e^{ikx}\,dk.
$$
These two equations are very similar in form to the Fourier series equations, but now we have integrals over all possible wave numbers $k$ instead of sums over discrete wavelengths.

::: {.callout-tip}
You can think of the Fourier transform as a continuous version of the Fourier series. Instead of summing over discrete wavelengths, we integrate over all possible wave numbers. The Fourier transform tells us how much of each wave number is present in our function, just like the Fourier coefficients tell us how much of each sine and cosine is present in a periodic function.
:::

## Basic properties
The Fourier transform has several algebraic properties that make it easy to manipulate functions before doing any integration.

### Linearity
If $f(x)$ has Fourier transform $\hat f(k)$ and $g(x)$ has Fourier transform $\hat g(k)$, then
$$
\widehat{af+bg}(k) = a\hat f(k) + b\hat g(k),
$$
for any constants $a$ and $b$.

This follows directly from the linearity of the integral:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\widehat{af+bg}(k)
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty (af(x)+bg(x))e^{-ikx}\,dx \\
&= a\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x)e^{-ikx}\,dx \\
&\qquad + b\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty g(x)e^{-ikx}\,dx \\
&= a\hat f(k)+b\hat g(k).
\end{aligned}
$$

### Scaling
If $a\neq 0$, then the Fourier transform of $f(ax)$ is
$$
\widehat{f(ax)}(k) = \frac{1}{|a|}\hat f\!\left(\frac{k}{a}\right).
$$

To show this, start from
$$
\widehat{f(ax)}(k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(ax)e^{-ikx}\,dx,
$$
and make the substitution $u=ax$, so $dx=du/a$. If $a<0$, the limits reverse, which produces the absolute value. This gives the stated result.^[If $a>0$, this is simply $\widehat{f(ax)}(k)=\frac{1}{a}\hat f(k/a)$.]

So compressing a function in real space stretches its Fourier transform in frequency space, and vice versa.

### Shifts in $x$
If we shift the function in real space, then
$$
\widehat{f(x-a)}(k) = e^{-ika}\hat f(k).
$$

This is straightforward to show using the substitution $u=x-a$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\widehat{f(x-a)}(k)
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x-a)e^{-ikx}\,dx \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(u)e^{-ik(u+a)}\,du \\
&= e^{-ika}\hat f(k).
\end{aligned}
$$

### Multiplication by a complex exponential
Multiplying by $e^{iax}$ shifts the Fourier transform:
$$
\widehat{e^{iax}f(x)}(k) = \hat f(k-a).
$$

This follows because
$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{iax}f(x)e^{-ikx}\,dx
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x)e^{-i(k-a)x}\,dx.
$$

::: {.callout-important}
The most useful basic rules are:

- $\widehat{af+bg}(k)=a\hat f(k)+b\hat g(k)$
- $\widehat{f(ax)}(k)=\frac{1}{|a|}\hat f(k/a)$ for $a\neq 0$
- $\widehat{f(x-a)}(k)=e^{-ika}\hat f(k)$
- $\widehat{e^{iax}f(x)}(k)=\hat f(k-a)$

These let us recycle transforms we already know instead of recomputing integrals from scratch.
:::

## Parity and the real/imaginary parts
Using
$$
e^{-ikx} = \cos(kx) - i\sin(kx),
$$
we can write the Fourier transform as
$$
\hat f(k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x)\cos(kx)\,dx - \frac{i}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x)\sin(kx)\,dx.
$$
So the real part of $\hat f(k)$ comes from the cosine integral, and the imaginary part comes from the sine integral:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Re}(\hat f(k)) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x)\cos(kx)\,dx, \\
\operatorname{Im}(\hat f(k)) &= -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x)\sin(kx)\,dx.
\end{aligned}
$$

Now recall that $\cos(kx)$ is an even function of $x$, while $\sin(kx)$ is odd. This gives some very useful symmetry rules.

- If $f$ is even, then $f(x)\cos(kx)$ is even and $f(x)\sin(kx)$ is odd. Therefore the sine integral is zero, so $\hat f(k)$ is purely real. In fact, $\hat f(k)$ is also even as a function of $k$.
- If $f$ is odd, then $f(x)\cos(kx)$ is odd and $f(x)\sin(kx)$ is even. Therefore the cosine integral is zero, so $\hat f(k)$ is purely imaginary. In fact, $\hat f(k)$ is also odd as a function of $k$.

More generally, any function can be split into its even and odd parts:
$$
f(x) = f_e(x) + f_o(x),
$$
where
$$
f_e(x) = \frac{f(x)+f(-x)}{2}, \qquad f_o(x) = \frac{f(x)-f(-x)}{2}.
$$
Then the Fourier transform of the even part contributes the real part, and the Fourier transform of the odd part contributes the imaginary part.

::: {.callout-important}
For a real-valued function $f$, the parity of $f$ tells us immediately what sort of Fourier transform we get:

- If $f$ is even, then $\hat f(k)$ is purely real and even.
- If $f$ is odd, then $\hat f(k)$ is purely imaginary and odd.
- More generally, the even part of $f$ contributes to the real part of $\hat f$, and the odd part contributes to the imaginary part.

This is often the quickest way to simplify a Fourier transform before doing any integration.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
So if $f$ is even, it is often enough to compute a cosine integral, and if $f$ is odd, it is often enough to compute a sine integral.
:::

## Example
Find the Fourier transform of
$$
f(x) = e^{-\frac{x^2}{2}}.
$$

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution
We have
$$
\hat f(k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{-\frac{x^2}{2}} e^{-ikx}\,dx.
$$
Combining the exponents gives
$$
\hat f(k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{-\frac{x^2}{2} - ikx}\,dx = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{-\frac{x^2 + 2ikx}{2}}\,dx.
$$
Completing the square in the exponent:
$$
x^2 + 2ikx = (x + ik)^2 + k^2,
$$
so
$$
\hat f(k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{-\frac{(x + ik)^2 + k^2}{2}}\,dx = \frac{e^{-\frac{k^2}{2}}}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{-\frac{(x + ik)^2}{2}}\,dx.
$$
Now we can make the substitution $u = x + ik$, so $du = dx$. The limits of integration remain $-\infty$ to $\infty$ since shifting by a constant (even a complex one) doesn't change them. Thus,
$$
\hat f(k) = \frac{e^{-\frac{k^2}{2}}}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{-\frac{u^2}{2}}\,du.
$$
The integral is just the standard Gaussian integral, which evaluates to $\sqrt{2\pi}$. Therefore,
$$
\hat f(k) = \frac{e^{-\frac{k^2}{2}}}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2\pi} = e^{-\frac{k^2}{2}}.
$$
So the Fourier transform of $f(x) = e^{-\frac{x^2}{2}}$ is
$$
\hat f(k) = e^{-\frac{k^2}{2}}.
$$
:::

### Example
Find the inverse Fourier transform of
$$
\hat  f(k) = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } |k| \leq 1, \\
0 & \text{if } |k| > 1.
\end{cases}
$$

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Solution
We have
$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^\infty \hat f(k) e^{ikx}\,dk = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-1}^1 e^{ikx}\,dk.
$$
We can integrate this directly:
$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\left[ \frac{e^{ikx}}{ix} \right]_{-1}^1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\left( \frac{e^{ix} - e^{-ix}}{ix} \right) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}\frac{\sin x}{x}.
$$
for $x\neq 0$. At $x=0$, we use the limit $\sin x/x \to 1$, so $f(0)=\sqrt{2/\pi}$.
:::
